content_en:
The Excellence of Knowledge is a work by the great Andalusian scholar Ibn 'Abd al-Barr al-Maliki (d. 463 AH / 1071 CE), one of the towering figures of Islamic hadith scholarship and one of the greatest scholars the Maghrib has produced. This text is a celebrated extract from his encyclopedic work Jami' Bayan al-'Ilm wa Fadlih (The Comprehensive Exposition of Knowledge and Its Excellence), which remains one of the most important classical texts on the obligation of seeking knowledge, its virtues, its etiquettes, and its categories.

The seeking of knowledge in Islam is not merely encouraged — it is obligatory. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Seeking knowledge is an obligation upon every Muslim." (Ibn Majah, hasan). The Quran repeatedly commands reflection, observation, and understanding: "Are those who know equal to those who do not know?" (Al-Zumar 39:9). The very first word revealed was "Iqra'" — Read! — establishing knowledge as the foundation of the prophetic mission itself.

Ibn 'Abd al-Barr's work establishes through extensive Quranic evidence, hadith, and the statements of the Companions and scholars why knowledge — particularly religious knowledge — is the highest pursuit available to a Muslim. He discusses: which category of knowledge is fard 'ayn (obligatory on every individual); which is fard kifayah (obligatory on the community collectively); the virtues of scholars and their status in Islam; the etiquettes of seeking knowledge; the obligation of acting on what one knows; the danger of knowledge that does not produce righteous action; and the relationship between knowledge, action, and sincerity.

This work has been read and studied for a thousand years as an inspiration for students of knowledge and as a reminder of why the path of learning is among the most beloved paths to Allah.
